title: [JENKINS-33704] Limit scope of `Jenkins#updateComputerList` to improve performance at scale
what_tried: The agent attempted to fix the performance issue by adding new targeted methods (`removeComputerForNode` and `updateComputerForNode`) to `AbstractCIBase` and corresponding wrapper methods in `Jenkins.java`. The approach was to create specialized single-node operations as alternatives to the full `updateComputerList`, rather than modifying the existing method signature.
why_wrong: The agent's approach diverged fundamentally from the ground truth fix. The correct fix modifies `updateComputerList` to accept a `Collection<Node>` parameter, limiting its scope to only the specified nodes rather than all nodes. The agent instead tried to bolt on new methods while leaving the existing broad `updateComputerList` intact, which means call sites that previously triggered a full update would still do so unless explicitly changed to use the new methods — failing to address the root cause at the architectural level.
missing_knowledge: A Jenkins contributor would know that the right pattern for scoping an existing broad operation is to parameterize the existing method rather than adding parallel methods, because the existing call sites in `Nodes.java` and elsewhere need to be updated to pass only the affected nodes. The contributor would also understand the `Queue.withLock` + `updateComputer` + `killComputer` flow well enough to know how to filter which computers get updated inside the lock, and would recognize that `nodes.contains(node)` is the correct predicate to limit scope rather than creating entirely separate code paths.

The contributor would also know Jenkins conventions around `@CheckForNull` from FindBugs/SpotBugs annotations to document nullable returns, and that enum ordering matters for readability/convention (new 'escape hatch' values go last). The agent over-engineered the solution by modifying additional files unnecessarily.

Do not compute CSP header if disabled
what_tried: The agent attempted to fix the unnecessary CSP header computation by moving the `isResourceRequest` check before the `headerValue` computation, and then using a ternary operator to conditionally compute `headerValue` only when `headerShouldBeSet` is true (returning `null` otherwise). This partially addressed the issue of skipping computation when CSP is disabled.
why_wrong: The agent's fix still computes the header value even for resource requests (when `isResourceRequest` is true), because it only guards on `headerShouldBeSet` but not on `!isResourceRequest`. The ground truth fix moves the computation inside the `if (headerShouldBeSet && !isResourceRequest)` block entirely, and also initializes `headerValue` to an empty string `""` instead of `null`, which matters for the `hasUnexpectedDifference` comparison logic later. Additionally, the agent's fix doesn't update the second condition in the `finally` block (`if (headerShouldBeSet)` → `if (headerShouldBeSet && !isResourceRequest)`) and doesn't simplify the redundant `!isResourceRequest` check inside it.
missing_knowledge: A Jenkins contributor would know that resource requests (served via `ResourceDomainConfiguration`) intentionally should not have CSP headers set, so computing the header value for them is also wasteful and incorrect. They would also understand that initializing `headerValue` to `""` (empty string) rather than `null` is semantically important for the `hasUnexpectedDifference` method's comparison logic, since the method likely handles empty strings and nulls differently. Furthermore, a contributor would recognize that the `finally` block's condition also needed updating to be consistent with the new guard logic, ensuring the diagnostic logging path is also gated on `!isResourceRequest`.

[JENKINS-73835] Do not allow builds to be deleted while they are still running and ensure build discarders run after builds are fully complete
what_tried: The agent attempted to prevent deletion of running builds by adding a check inside the existing `synchronized(this)` block in the `delete()` method. It used `isBuilding()` as the condition to detect a running build and placed the check after the `isPendingDelete` guard, throwing an IOException if the build was still running.
why_wrong: The agent used `isBuilding()` instead of `isLogUpdated()` as the condition, and placed the check inside the `synchronized` block rather than before it. The correct fix uses `isLogUpdated()` (which checks if the build log is still being updated, a more accurate indicator of an incomplete build) and places it *before* the `synchronized` block. Additionally, the agent failed to remove the `logRotate()` call from the `execute()` method's completion logic, which was the other half of the fix needed to avoid race conditions where the build discarder could run before the build was fully complete.
missing_knowledge: A Jenkins contributor would know that `isLogUpdated()` is a more precise signal than `isBuilding()` for whether a build is truly still in-progress (covering edge cases like post-build finalization), and that `logRotate()` being called inside `execute()` before `onEndBuilding()` creates a race condition where build discarders can act on builds not yet fully finalized. The contributor would also understand the relationship between `BackgroundGlobalBuildDiscarder` and the `logRotate()` call, knowing that removing the in-execution `logRotate()` call is necessary because the background discarder handles rotation after builds complete.

title: [JENKINS-73835] Do not allow builds to be deleted while they are still running and ensure build discarders run after builds are fully complete
what_tried: The agent attempted to fix the bug by adding a check in the `DeleteBuildsCommand.act()` method (the CLI delete-builds command handler) to reject deletion of running builds by checking `build.isLogUpdated()` before calling `build.delete()`. This is a surface-level fix at the CLI command layer.
why_wrong: The agent fixed only one entry point (the CLI command) rather than fixing the root cause in `Run.delete()` itself, which is the correct defensive location. The ground truth fix adds the guard inside `Run.delete()` so that ALL callers (REST API, CLI, LogRotator, plugins, etc.) are protected. The agent also missed the second part of the fix: removing the `getParent().logRotate()` call from within the build's finishing sequence, which was needed to prevent build discarders from running before builds are fully complete.
missing_knowledge: A Jenkins contributor would know to apply defensive checks at the lowest-level shared method (`Run.delete()`) rather than at individual callers, because Jenkins has many entry points (REST API via `doDelete`, CLI, internal callers) and patching each one is fragile. They would also understand the build lifecycle subtlety that `logRotate()` was being called inside the build completion block before `onEndBuilding()`, meaning build discarders could race with builds still wrapping up — and that this call needed to be moved or removed entirely, with `BackgroundGlobalBuildDiscarder` or `GlobalBuildDiscarderListener` handling post-build cleanup instead.

missing_knowledge: A Jenkins contributor would know that `@StaplerNotDispatchable` marks a method as not directly dispatchable by Stapler (meaning it shouldn't be called via HTTP dispatch at all in the default interface method context), while `@RequirePOST` restricts dispatch to POST requests only — these have very different semantics.
